segments.s688-g129: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that' / 'that'), here leading into what was to be done. نام برنا و ده functions as the content of that command: نام برنا = 'the name
segments.s688-g130: نویسد is a 3rd-person subjunctive/jussive verb ('may/should write'), used after تا to mark the commanded action; classical/poetic word order places the verb before its agent. نویسنده روزبه is the postposed agent ('the scribe Ruzbeh'), with نویسنده specifying the role and روزبه the proper name.
segments.s688-g131: ببازارگان is the prepositional phrase 'to the merchants' (به + بازارگان) indicating the addressees. گفت is the past verb 'said' introducing the reported speech, and پدرود باش is an imperative formula ('be farewell' / 'be greeted' — here functioning as a farewell greeting).
segments.s688-g132: خرد را uses the object marker را to present 'wisdom' as the definite thing concerned. به دل ('in/to the heart') gives the locus; تار و هم پود is the fixed pair 'warp and (also) weft' (هم stresses the second member), and باش is an imperative meaning 'let it be' or 'be so' — together: 'let wisdom be the warp and weft of the heart.'
segments.s688-g57: This is a prepositional/verb-object cluster placed before the verb: بر آتش (‘on the fire’) + پراگند (‘scattered’ as adjective/participle) + چندی کباب (‘some/several kebabs’). Word order is poetic: the phrase describes where and what was laid on the fire before the action of eating is stated in the next segment.
segments.s688-g58: گرفتند is a past tense verb meaning ‘they took/seized’ used here in the idiom گرفتند به + infinitive = ‘they began / took to (doing)’. بخوردن is the infinitive ‘to eat’; یاران is the subject ‘the companions’; شتاب is a noun ‘haste’ used adverbially (‘with haste’). Together: ‘the companions took to eating with haste.’
segments.s688-g59: گرفتند is the past plural ‘they seized/took’; واژ is an archaic/poetic verb/adverb meaning ‘overturned/threw down’. آنک introduces a relative clause ‘who/that’; بد qualifies دین‌پژوه as ‘bad/ill‑disposed’; دین‌پژوه is ‘religious scholar/theologian’. The clause thus describes the object they seized: ‘they seized and overturned him who was a bad theologian.’
segments.s688-g60: بخوردن is again the infinitive ‘to eat’. شتابید is the past tense ‘they hurried’ (i.e., made haste); دیگر modifies گروه as ‘another / the other’ group; گروه is the noun ‘group/company’. The construction means ‘another group hurried to eat’ (infinitive indicating purpose or immediate action).
segments.s688-g61: بخوردند is past ‘they ate’. بی‌نان (‘without bread’) is an adverbial/prepositional phrase describing how they ate. فراوان modifies کباب (‘abundant/plentiful kebab’) and functions adjectivally. The segment states the action and circumstances: ‘they ate abundant kebab without bread.’
segments.s688-g62: بیاراست is a past/action verb meaning ‘(he/they) prepared/arranged’ (poetic form of آراستن). هر مهتری is the subject phrase ‘each/ every chief’ (هر = each; مهتری = chief/elder). جای خواب is the object ‘place of sleep’ (= sleeping place/bed). Word order is slightly marked but reads: ‘each chief prepared a sleeping place.’
segments.s688-g63: زمانی here means ‘for a while’ (temporal adverbial). بخفتند is past plural ‘they slept’. و is the conjunction ‘and’. برخاستند is past plural ‘they rose up/they got up’. The sequence gives a short narrative: they slept awhile and then rose.
segments.s688-g64: یکی functions as ‘one’/‘someone’ (indefinite subject). آفرین is a noun ‘acclamation/praise’ (an applause or exclamation of praise). نو is the adjective ‘new’. آراستند is past plural of آراستن ‘they fashioned/arranged/ composed’ used here with آفرین as its object: ‘one of them composed a new acclamation.’
segments.s688-g25: همی‌تاخت is an older/poetic imperfective verb form meaning 'kept charging' or 'was riding/advancing' (verb before its complements). تا here introduces an extent ('as far as/until'), and پیش آب فرات is a prepositional/locative phrase 'before the water (i.e. the) Euphrates' with پیش = 'in front of/before' and آب فرات = 'the River Euphrates'.
segments.s688-g26: ندید is the past (negative) form 'did not see' (ن‑ + دید). اندرو is a fused classical locative 'in/within it/there' referring back to the ground they reached. هیچ جای نبات is a negated noun phrase: هیچ modifying جای ('no/none of the place') and نبات = 'vegetation/plant life'; together 'no place of vegetation'.
segments.s688-g27: شده گرسنه is a participial/state expression 'having become hungry' (شده = 'become', گرسنه = 'hungry'). The following noun phrase مرد پیر وجوان lists who: 'men, old and young' (word order places the state before the noun, a common poetic ordering).
segments.s688-g28: یکی بیشه دیدند is a simple past clause 'they saw a certain grove' where یکی = 'a certain' (indefinite), بیشه = 'grove/thicket', دیدند = 'they saw' (past, 3rd pl.). و آب روان attaches a coordinated noun phrase 'and running/flowing water' (آب = water, روان = 'flowing').
segments.s688-g29: چوخسرو is the fused classical circumstantial 'چو + خسرو' meaning 'when Khosrow' (introducing the temporal clause). به پیش اندرون بیشه is a locative phrase 'toward/in front, into the interior of the grove' where به = 'toward/into', پیش = 'ahead/in front of', اندرون = 'inside/within', بیشه = 'grove'. دید is the past 'saw'.
segments.s688-g30: سپه را is the definite object/topic 'the army' marked by را. بران is an adverbial/demonstrative 'onto/thereupon' (on/at that place). سبزه اندر کشید is a verbal phrase where سبزه = 'green place/grass', اندر = 'into', and کشید is the past of کشیدن 'drew/dragged/led' — here 'led/dragged (the army) into the green spot'. (Note: classical/lexical variants of کشیدن are used frequently to mean 'bring/lead'.)
segments.s688-g31: شده گرسنه mirrors the earlier structure 'having become hungry' (شده = 'become', گرسنه = 'hungry'). مرد here denotes the persons (men); ناهاروسست is an older compound meaning 'it was noon / midday had come' or 'it was time for the midday meal' (a temporal note explaining why they were hungry).
segments.s688-g32: کمان را is the object 'the bow' (marked by را). بزه کرد is a verb cluster where کرد is the light verb 'did/made' and بزه is an older/less common word here used with the bow (the exact lexical nuance is archaic/poetic); syntactically this yields 'he used/did [something] with the bow'. نخچیر جست is a separate short clause 'the prey/game sprang away' (نخچیر = game/prey, جست = 'leapt/sprang/fled').
segments.s688-g9: همان چون together functions like 'as soon as / just when'; همان here is an emphatic 'just/at that very' and چون = 'when/as'. فرود آمد is a past verb phrase 'descended / came down' (فرود as the nominal/verb element + آمد). اندر زمان is a temporal prepositional phrase 'at that moment' (اندر = 'in/at', زمان = 'moment/time'). Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal phrase follows the verb to mark the exact time.
segments.s688-g10: نوندی is used as a substantive referring to the Navand musical mode or a Navand strain/performer (subject). بیامد is the past tense 'came' (the b‑prefix is common in older/poetic forms). ز ایران دمان is a place expression 'from the skirts/outskirts of Iran' (ز = 'from', ایران = place, دمان = 'hem/skirt, here 'outskirts').
segments.s688-g11: ز before a name means 'from' (poetic/genitive source). بهرام چوبین here names the sender; because the provided senses list doesn't include the proper‑name reading for چوبین, it is treated as a name (see senses field). یکی نامه داشت = 'he had one letter' or 'a certain letter' (یکی as indefinite 'a certain/one', نامه = 'letter', داشت = past 'had').
segments.s688-g12: همان نامه = 'that same letter' (demonstrative reference back to the previous clause). پوشیده is a past participle/adjective 'hidden/covered' modifying نامه; در جامه = 'in (his) garment' (در = 'in', جامه = 'garment'), and داشت is the past auxiliary 'had' — literal order is 'that letter hidden in a garment he had', i.e. 'he kept that letter hidden in his clothing.'
segments.s688-g13: نوشته is used as a noun 'a written message' or 'the writing' (here the letter/notice); سوی = 'to/toward' marks the recipient; مهتری = 'the chief/elder' is the addressee. باهله is an obscure/rare item in the line (likely a name or an adverbial epithet); the sense is uncertain in the lexical list and is treated here as an obscure modifier.
segments.s688-g14: که introduces reported speech or the content of the written order ('that' / 'saying that'). گرلشکر is a fused conditional: گر = 'if' + لشکر = 'army' → 'if an army'. آید is the present/future sense 'comes' (classical Persian often uses present to express a future contingency). مکنشان is an imperative negative formed from مکن ('do not') with the object clitic شان ('them') attached (meaning 'do not (let) them...'). یله is the verb/complement meaning 'loose/release/slacken' — together مکنشان یله = 'do not let them loose / do not release them.'
segments.s688-g15: سپاه من = 'my army' (possessive order: noun + pronoun). اینک = 'now' (adverb). پس here has an older sense 'behind / in reserve / at the rear' (used adverbially) rather than simply 'then'; اندر دمان = 'in the skirts/foothills' — a locative phrase. The overall order places time/adverbial and location after the subject in typical classical style.
segments.s688-g16: بشهر تو = 'to your city' (بِ + شهر with the enclitic pronoun تو 'your'). آید is the present/future sense 'will come' (classical present used for near future). زمان تا زمان is a repetitive/time‑intensive phrase meaning 'moment to moment / at any moment' (تا is here functioning to link the repeated زمان and produce the idiom).
segments.s688-g89: This noun phrase names the subject: 'یکی' functions as an indefinite 'a certain/another', followed by the head noun 'کاروان' (caravan). The two particles 'نیز دیگر' are additive/emphatic ('also/another') placed after the noun for poetic emphasis. 'به راه' is a locative phrase 'on/along the road' following the noun phrase, preparing the scene for the verb in the next segment.
segments.s688-g90: 'پدید آمد' is a past-tense verbal expression 'appeared/emerged' (پدید + آمد). The following adverbials 'از دور' ('from afar') and 'پیش سپاه' ('before/in front of the army') give source and location; word order places the verb first and then its circumstantial phrases, a common poetic arrangement that links back to the subject in the previous segment.
segments.s688-g91: A nominal identification: 'یکی' = 'a certain', 'مرد بازارگان' = 'merchant man' (بازارگان is an older/poetic form of 'merchant'), and the adjective 'مایه‌دار' ('wealthy') follows the noun as is normal in Persian; together they mean 'a certain wealthy merchant.'
segments.s688-g92: 'بیامد' is the past tense 'he came' (3rd singular). The particles 'هم آنگه' act together as an adverbial: 'also/and then' or 'straightaway/at once', adding immediacy. 'بر شهریار' uses the preposition 'بر' in the sense 'to/toward' plus 'شهریار' (the king), meaning he approached/advanced to the king; the whole segment gives the action of the subject from the previous segment.
segments.s688-g93: 'بدو گفت شاه' is an inverted poetic order meaning 'the king said to him' (بدو = 'to him'). 'کز' here introduces the content/origin question ('from where'), and 'کجایی بگوی' is an imperative/subjunctive clause 'tell (me) where you are/from where (you come)'. Note that 'بگوی' is an older/poetic form of the verb 'say/tell' used to introduce reported speech.
segments.s688-g94: 'کجا رفت خواهی' shows a metrical/inverted order: verb 'رفت' (go) appears before the modal auxiliary 'خواهی' (you will/wish), together meaning 'where will you go?' or 'where do you intend to go?'. 'چنین پوی پوی' is an adverbial phrase of manner: 'so/thus, running swiftly' with 'پوی' repeated for emphasis — 'so very swift/rapidly.'
segments.s688-g95: 'بدو گفت' again = 'he said to him'. 'کز' is a subordinating particle 'that/from which/from' introducing the reported answer or origin. 'خره اردشیر' is a compound in which 'اردشیر' is a proper name; the exact meaning of 'خره' here is lexical/possibly toponymic or archaic, but grammatically this whole phrase supplies the source/identifier introduced by 'کز'.
segments.s688-g96: This is reported self-identification. 'یکی' = 'a certain', 'مرد' = 'man', and 'بازارگانم' combines بازارگان (merchant) with the enclitic '-م', functioning as the copula 'I am' in direct speech ('I am a merchant'). 'دبیر' follows in apposition = 'a scribe/secretary.' Together the clause reads as the speaker saying 'I am a certain merchant, (a) scribe.'
segments.s688-g1: همی‌تاخت is a past-progressive/iterative verb meaning 'was charging' (literary/past continuous). The word order places the verb before the subject here for poetic effect: 'همی‌تاخت خسرو' = 'Khosrow was charging'. به پیش اندرون is a prepositional/locative phrase ('towards/into the front/inner part') with به + پیش + اندرون indicating motion into the interior.
segments.s688-g2: نه ... و نه ... is a correlative negative construction meaning 'neither ... nor ...'. بود is the past copula 'was'. The phrase states two absent things: 'no water and no guidance.'
segments.s688-g3: عنان را is the direct-object/topic phrase 'the reins' marked by را. بدان باره is an adverbial phrase 'for/on that occasion' (بدان = 'to/for that' in older usage, باره = 'occasion'). کرده یله is a light-perfect/causative construction: کرده (made/done) + یله (loose), together 'had made (them) loose' = 'had loosened the reins.' Word order puts the object and adverbial before the verb phrase.
segments.s688-g4: همی‌راند is another past-progressive verb 'was urging/driving (the horse) on.' ناکام here is an adverbial/adjectival comment 'in vain/unsuccessfully.' تا introduces an extent 'as far as/until' and باهله is a place-name or adverbial destination following تا, so the whole clause means he rode on vainly up to Bahaleh.
segments.s688-g5: پذیره is a noun/participial form meaning 'reception' or 'acceptance' and here functions as part of a verbal phrase. شدندش is an older sculpted verbal form meaning 'they became/they received him' (شدند + ـش object suffix), so the segment reads 'the great ones of the city took/received him' with بزرگان شهر as the agent phrase placed before the verb.
segments.s688-g6: کسی را که introduces a relative clause 'someone who ...' (کسی را = 'someone' as a topicalized object/subject phrase), از مردمی بود is the relative clause 'was from the people / belonged to the common folk' (از = 'from/of', مردمی = 'of the people', بود = past 'was'). بهر at the end is the older preposition 'for/to/for the sake of' and here signals the clause's purpose/link to what follows.
segments.s688-g7: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' خسرو is the subject. به نزدیک ایشان is a locative phrase 'near to them' (به + نزدیک + ایشان), and رسید is the past tense 'arrived/approached.' Together: 'When Khosrow approached them.'
segments.s688-g8: بران شهر is a prepositional phrase 'upon that city' (بران = 'upon/that'), لشکر فرود آورید is a verbal complex 'they caused the army to descend/they brought the army down' — فرود (descent) + آوردید (brought, past plural). The verb is past plural, so the implied agent is 'they' (the elders or allies).
segments.s688-g81: چو(dastur) باشی is a conditional phrase: چو (here fused with دستور) = “if / as (you) order,” and باشی is a subjunctive/present form of بودن used in classical Persian to mark the condition (“if it be/if you order”). من is the subject “I” that follows the protasis. ازگوشت و آب is a prepositional phrase (‘of meat and water’), here marking the supplies the speaker will bring.
segments.s688-g82: به راه آورم is the apodosis: به راه (= ‘on/to the road’) + آورم, first‑person present/subjunctive used poetically for future (‘I will bring/carry’). گر introduces a conditional/exhortative ‘if’ (often = “unless/if”) and نسازی is the negative second‑person present/subjunctive of ساختن (‘you do not make/do not do’); شتاب is a noun ‘haste’ so نسازی شتاب means ‘if you do not make haste / if you do not hurry.’
segments.s688-g83: بدو گفت خسرو is an inverted free word‑order common in poetry: بدو = ‘to him,’ گفت is the past‑tense verb ‘said’ (here used with the dative), and خسرو is the speaker’s name. جزین نیست رای is an idiom: جز = ‘except,’ این = ‘this,’ نیست = ‘is not’ (negating alternatives), and رای = ‘counsel/plan,’ so the clause means ‘there is no counsel except this.’
segments.s688-g84: که introduces a content/clause meaning ‘that.’ با توشه باشیم is a purposive/subjunctive clause: با = ‘with,’ توشه = ‘provisions,’ and باشیم is the first‑person plural subjunctive of بودن used here like a hortatory/subjunctive ‘we should be / let us be.’ و با رهنمای repeats با + noun for ‘and with a guide,’ balancing the objects of باشیم.
segments.s688-g85: هیونی is the direct object ‘a beast / wild animal.’ بر افگند is the past tense verb phrase: بر + افگند (‘cast upon’ / ‘threw at’), where بر marks the direction/target. تازی is the human target ‘the Arab,’ and به راه locates the action ‘onto/into the road.’ The verb precedes its directional phrase in this poetic order.
segments.s688-g86: بدان is an adverbial/concatenated particle meaning ‘thereupon / with that’ (classical poetic contraction of ب‑ + آن). تا here introduces a result/consequence (‘so that’), and برد is the past of بردن used transitively/iteratively meaning ‘took / made (a movement onto).’ راه پیش سپاه is a noun phrase ‘the road before/in front of the army,’ where پیش = ‘before / in front of’ modifies سپاه ‘army.’
segments.s688-g87: همی‌تاخت is an iterative/imperfective past verb meaning ‘was charging / kept rushing’ (همی‑ is a habitual/progressive prefix). اندر is the archaic preposition ‘in’ and introduces the locative objects بیابان و کوه (‘desert and mountain’), which are coordinated by و.
segments.s688-g88: پر از is an idiom meaning ‘full of’ (پر = full, followed by the preposition از = ‘of’). رنج و تیمار lists the qualities ‘hardship and care,’ and با آن گروه is a prepositional phrase ‘with that troop/group,’ where آن = ‘that’ points back to the previously mentioned company.
segments.s688-g33: ندیدند is the past 3rd‑person plural of دیدن (here meaning 'they saw'); in classical poetic word order the verb can precede its object. چیزی is the indefinite object 'something'. بجایی is an adverbial phrase (بِ + جا) meaning 'at the place' or 'to the spot', and دوان (participle/active adjective from دویدن) modifies چیزی as 'running/rushing' (i.e. 'something running to/at the place').
segments.s688-g34: درخت و گیا بود is a simple nominal clause with بود as the past copula 'there was/were' (3rd singular used existentially); the conjunction و links درخت and گیا ('trees and plants'). و آب روان continues the description: آب 'water' modified by روان as an adjective 'flowing/streaming'.
segments.s688-g35: پدید آمد is a compound verbal idea meaning 'appeared' (پدید 'apparent/visible' + آمد 'came'); اندر زمان is a temporal prepositional phrase 'in the time/at that moment', and کاروان is the subject placed after the verb as is common in poetry.
segments.s688-g36: شتر بود uses بود as an existential 'there were camels' (شتر 'camel'). و links this to the following locative/positional phrase; پیش اندرون together indicates position 'in front/at the head of the inner part', and ساروان (plural) names the people there 'camel‑drivers/caravan leaders'.
segments.s688-g37: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; آن ساربان is the subject 'that camel‑driver'. روی خسرو is the direct object phrase 'the face/appearance of Khosrow' (روی = face/appearance + proper name in genitive relation), and بدید is the past tense 'saw'.
segments.s688-g38: بدان here is the contraction of به آن ('to that/unto him') functioning as a dative; نامدار is an epithet 'renowned'. آفرین is a noun 'praise/acclamation' and گسترید is the past tense 'he spread/scattered' used metaphorically for 'uttered/bestowed' praise on the person mentioned.
segments.s688-g39: بدو is the dative phrase 'to him' (به او). گفت is the verb 'said' (here introducing direct speech); خسرو is the speaker. که introduces the indirect question clause, and نام تو چیست is the fused question 'what is your name?' with نام 'name' and توچیست functioning as 'what is your...' (چیست = 'is what').
segments.s688-g40: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. رفت خواهی is an inverted poetic order of the future/intentional construction (خواهی رفت = 'you will go / are going'); here the past stem رفت is followed by the modal/auxiliary خواهی. و links the second question: کام تو چیست literally 'what is your desire?' with کام 'desire', تو the 2nd‑person pronoun, and ست/چی(ست) forming the copular question 'what is'.
segments.s688-g97: This is an interrogative clause. بدو گفـت (literally "he said to him") is a common word order (indir. speech marker + گفت). نامت (your-name with the suffixed -ت) is the direct object; چه کرد is a light-question construction where چه (what) is followed by کرد (past of کردن, "to make/give") meaning "what did (he) make/assign (as your name)". آنک زاد is a relative nominal phrase: آنک (who/that) + زاد (past of زادن, “to beget”) = "the one who begot you." Word-for-word: "He said to him: 'Your name what did the one who begot (you) do?'" — i.e. "What name did your begetter give you?"
segments.s688-g98: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase meaning "he gave this reply" (چنین = thus/so + داد = gave + پاسخ = answer). The conjunction که introduces the content of the reply. مهران is the proper-name answer. ستاد is a past tense verb (here used predicatively after the name) and in older/poetic Persian can function to give a laudatory nuance; taken together the phrase reports the reply as a short predicate following the name ("Mehrān [stated/was praised]").
segments.s688-g99: ازو توشه جست is a compact clause in which ازو (from him/of him) precedes the object توشه (provisions, bundle). جست is a past form; here it conveys the action "seized/took" or broadly "took (away) a provision." آن زمان شهریار is a temporal + subject tag: آن‌زمان (at that time) + شهریار (the king) — giving the agent/time of the action.
segments.s688-g100: بدو گفت is again the reporting phrase "he said to him." سالار کای نامدار is a vocative phrase addressing the addressee: سالار (chief/commander) + کای (vocative marker "O") + نامدار (renowned). In older Persian the vocative particle can follow the noun, so the whole segment is "he said to him, 'O renowned chief.'"
segments.s688-g101: خورش هست چندانک اندازه نیست is a descriptive clause. خورش هست = "there is stew" (خورش as subject, هست = copula/existential). چندانک (to that extent/so much) modifies the degree; اندازه نیست literally "there is no measure" (اندازه = measure/extent; نیست = is not), so the clause means "the stew is so plentiful that it cannot be measured."
segments.s688-g102: اگر introduces a condition ("if/provided that"). چهره بازارگان تازه نیست literally reads "the merchant's face is not new;" here چهره functions as "appearance" and بازارگان as "merchant," so the idiom means "if the merchant is not new-faced" (i.e. not a novice). The negative نیست applies to تازه (new/fresh).
segments.s688-g103: بدو گفت خسرو که is the reporting construction: بدو گفت (he said to him) + خسرو (Khosrow/the king) + که (that) introducing reported content. مهمان به راه places the potential guest as "a guest on the road" (important idiom for a traveler/wayfarer). Word order follows classical Persian: reporter + name + conjunction + reported clause.
segments.s688-g104: بیابی is a subjunctive/optative form of یافتن (to find) here used in indirect speech after گفت ("that you/one should find"); it conveys "if (one) finds" or "you should find." فزونی شود دستگاه is a result clause: فزونی (increase/excess) + شود (becomes/will be) + دستگاه (retinue/establishment/court). Together: "(if) a guest is found on the road, the court's retinue will increase."
segments.s688-g17: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when' introducing the clause; مهتر is the subject 'the elder/chief'; برانگونه is an adverbial modifier 'in that way/so' placed before the noun it modifies; برنامه is the direct object 'plan'; دید is the past-tense verb 'saw' that closes the clause. Word order is typical Persian (topic/adjuncts — object — verb) in poetic diction.
segments.s688-g18: هم اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase 'at the same time' (هم giving 'also/at once', اندر = 'in/at'); پیش خسرو is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before / in the presence of Khosrow'; دوید is the past verb 'ran/hastened'. Note the archaic preposition اندر used for 'in/at'.
segments.s688-g19: چو + name (چوخسرو) = 'when Khosrow...'; نگه کرد is a light verbal construction where نگه (gaze/look) + کرد (to do) = 'looked'; و connects the two actions; نامه بخواند places the object نامه before the verb بخواند 'read', a normal Persian order in poetic style.
segments.s688-g20: ز is the causal/prepositional particle 'because of / from'; کار جهان is a genitive-like phrase 'the affairs/works of the world'; در شگفتی بماند is a predicative phrase 'remained in astonishment' with در + noun indicating state and بماند (past-form used as resultive) meaning 'was left / remained'.
segments.s688-g21: بترسید is a past-tense verb 'they feared'; که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'; آید is the verb 'would come' (present-tense form used for future/hortative sense); پس here means 'after'/'following'; او سپاه = 'his army' (pronoun + noun). The clause thus expresses fear of a following army.
segments.s688-g22: بران is an adverbial 'thereupon / on that account'; نامه‌بر 'the letter‑carrier' is the subject; تنگدل is an adjective 'narrow‑hearted / melancholy' describing his state; گشت is the past verb 'became'; شاه at the end is a vocative address 'O king'. Word order places the adverbial front, then subject, predicate, and a final vocative.
segments.s688-g23: ازان شهر 'from that city' (ازان = 'from that'); هم در زمان 'also/at the same time' (هم = 'also', در زمان = 'at the time'); برنشست is a past action 'mounted/took his seat' (here 'rose/mounted' meaning set out). The sequence gives place + time + action.
segments.s688-g24: میان کیی = 'in the midst of the Kayanian/ Kiani host' (میان = 'among / middle of', کیی = 'Kayanian'); تاختن is a verbal noun 'the charge/attack' or action 'to charge'; را is the object marker marking تاختن as the definite object; ببست is the past verb 'he closed/blocked / stopped'. Together: 'he stopped the charge among the Kays.'
segments.s688-g121: ازان پس is an adverbial phrase 'after that / thereupon'; ببازارگان is the prepositional/indirect phrase naming the addressees ('to/for the merchants'); the sequence گفت شاه is a poetic inversion: گفت (said) + شاه (the king) — normally شاه گفت, but here the verb appears before the subject for metrical/poetic effect.
segments.s688-g122: که introduces the content of the king's utterance ('which'); اکنون 'now' sets time; سپه را is marked by the object/topic marker را (here marking 'the army' as the definite thing being asked about); کدامست (kadamast) is the interrogative copula 'which is it?'; راه is the predicate 'road/way' that pairs with کدامست. The clause literally asks 'which is now the road to the army?'.
segments.s688-g123: نشست تو is 'your seat/place of sitting' (نشست used as noun phrase 'seat/position' with the pronoun تو); در introduces the locative phrase 'in ...'; خره (here a locative noun) + اردشیر names the place associated with Ardashir. Word order is possessor/pronoun after the noun (نشست تو = 'your seat').
segments.s688-g124: کجا 'where' begins the question; باشد is the classical/polite third-person form of 'to be' used here in a ponderous/polite/questioning way (literary Persian often uses باشد where modern Persian would use است); ای is a vocative particle 'O'; مرد مهمان‌پذیر is the addressee 'hospitable man' (noun + adjectival compound). The phrase addresses someone and asks where his seat is.
segments.s688-g125: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it' marks the indirect object); کای شاه is a vocative address 'O king' (کای = O); با داد introduces a manner/instrument phrase 'with justice' (با + noun); ورای here is a compacted/formal item following داد — in classical lines such sequences can mean 'and counsel' or serve as a pronominal/locative element depending on context (the word is archaic/poetic). The whole segment is the opening of direct speech.
segments.s688-g126: ز بازارگانان literally 'from the merchants' or 'of the merchants' (ز = 'from/of'); منم is the emphatic present 'I am'; پاک is an adjective 'pure' or an adverbial intensifier here qualifying رای; رای is 'opinion/counsel' — together منم پاک رای expresses 'I am (of) pure counsel' or 'my counsel is pure.'
segments.s688-g127: نشانش 'his/its sign(s)' is the direct object/topic (the suffix -ش marks 'his/its'); یکایک 'one by one' is an adverb modifying the action; به خسرو marks the indirect object 'to Khosrow'; بگفت is the classical past/simple perfect third-person 'he said/told' (literary form where the preverb بـ- appears). The clause means 'he told Khosrow the signs one by one.'
segments.s688-g128: همه رازها 'all the secrets' is the subject/topic; برگشاد is a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'opened/revealed' (perfective/resultant); از نهفت is a compact phrase 'from concealment' (از = from, نهفت = hidden/concealed). Together the clause says the secrets were laid open, i.e., all the secrets were revealed.
segments.s688-g73: بما بیش باید که دارد امید: بَمـا (prepositional phrase) = 'for/to us' placed frontally; بیش باید = 'it is more necessary / we ought more' (بیش modifies باید: a comparative/intensifying sense). که introduces a subordinate clause. دارد (3sg present of داشتن) governs the noun امید ('has hope'). Together: 'For us it is more proper/necessary that (he) has hope' = 'we ought to have greater hope that...'.
segments.s688-g74: سراسر به نیکی دهیدش نوید: سراسر is an adverbial 'throughout/entirely'; به نیکی 'with/in goodness' qualifies the tidings. دهیدش is an address to second person (pl.) 'give him' (دهید + enclitic -ش), and نوید is the object 'good news/tidings'. Word order places the adverbial before the verb: 'give him tidings of goodness throughout.'
segments.s688-g75: گرفتند یاران برو آفرین: گرفتند (past 3pl) 'they took/raised' is used with یاران 'the companions' (subject) — inversion is poetic. برو and آفرین are interjections/imperatives: برو = 'go on/forward' and آفرین = 'bravo' (acclamation). The phrase describes the companions taking up that shout.
segments.s688-g76: که‌ای پاک‌دل خسرو پاک دین: که‌ای here functions as a vocative particle (literary 'که ای' = 'O'); پاک‌دل is a compound adjective 'pure‑hearted' placed before the noun; خسرو is the addressee/title 'king/sovereign'; پاک دین is an epithet 'pure in religion' (i.e. 'pure of faith'). The whole is a vocative: 'O pure‑hearted king, pure in faith.'
segments.s688-g77: بپرسید زان مرد تازی که راه: بپرسید is an imperative/address to listeners 'ask!' (classical form with ب- prefix plus پرسید). زان = 'of/from that' (pointing to the man), مرد تازی = 'that Arab man'. که introduces the embedded question 'which way...' (relative/interrogative marker).
segments.s688-g78: کدامست و من چون شوم با سپاه: کدامست = 'which is it?' (interrogative). و = 'and'. من = 'I' (subject). چون here is the interrogative 'how'; شوم is 1sg subjunctive/present of شدن 'shall I become / shall I be' used idiomatically 'how shall I fare/become'; با سپاه 'with the army' is a prepositional phrase modifying شوم. Together: 'which way is it, and how shall I fare with the army?'
segments.s688-g79: بدو گفت هفتاد فرسنگ بیش: بدو = 'to him' (enclitic prepositional pronoun). گفت is the past 3sg 'he said' (verb follows the prepositional object). هفتاد فرسنگ بیش = 'seventy parasangs (farsangs) more' — here بیش after the numeral gives the sense 'more than seventy' (i.e. 'over seventy').
segments.s688-g80: شما را بیابان و کوهست پیش: شما را = 'for you' / topicalized object 'you' with the object marker را. بیابان و کوهست = 'desert and mountain(s) are' (کوهست here functions to denote 'mountain(s)/is mountainous'); پیش = 'before/ahead'. Word order is topical/poetic: 'Before you is desert and mountain.'
segments.s688-g105: 'سر بار بگشاد بازارگان' is a small clause in which 'سر بار' (literally 'top/head of the load') is the object phrase placed before the verb; 'بگشاد' is a past verb (opened) and 'بازارگان' (merchants) is the subject, placed after the verb in this archaic/poetic word order.
segments.s688-g106: 'درمگان به آمد ز دینارگان' continues the action: 'درمگان' (the moneyed/wealthy people) is the subject; 'به آمد' is a compound past (came/approached) with the preposition-like particle 'به' indicating direction/approach; 'ز دینارگان' is a source phrase 'from the dinar‑holders.' The verb 'آمد' stands before the source phrase as expected in classical Persian.
segments.s688-g107: In 'خورش برد و بنشست خود بر زمین' the food 'خورش' appears before the verb 'برد' (past of بردن, 'took/took hold of') as the object; then the coordinated verb 'بنشست' ('sat down') follows. 'خود' is a reflexive/emphatic pronoun ('himself') and 'بر زمین' is the locative prepositional phrase 'on the ground.'
segments.s688-g108: 'همی‌خواند' is an imperfective/habitual form (the prefix همی- marks continuing or repeated action) meaning 'kept singing/crying out.' The phrase 'بر شهریار آفرین' uses 'بر' in the sense 'to/for' + 'شهریار' (the king) and 'آفرین' is a noun 'acclamation/praise' — together 'kept crying praise to the king.' Word order places the continuous verb before this prepositional phrase.
segments.s688-g109: 'چونان خورده شد' is a temporal/subordinate clause meaning 'when/just as it had been eaten' — 'خورده' is the past participle 'eaten' and 'شد' functions as the auxiliary/passive 'was'; 'مرد مهمان‌پرست' is the subject that follows this temporal clause ('the hospitable man').
segments.s688-g110: In 'بیامد گرفت آبدستان بدست' two past verbs are coordinated: 'بیامد' (he came) + 'گرفت' (he took/seized). 'آبدستان' is the direct object (cupbearer/water‑attendant) and 'بدست' is an instrumental/locative phrase 'by the hand' placed after the object.
segments.s688-g111: 'چو' is a temporal conjunction 'when'; the prepositional phrase 'از دور' ('from afar') precedes the name+epithet 'خراد برزین' (Barzin the Wise), and the past verb 'بدید' ('saw') comes at the end — a normal classical order for a temporal clause.
segments.s688-g112: 'ز جایی که ... دوید' is a relative/locative clause 'from a place from which ... he ran.' The sequence 'ز جایی که' introduces the origin/location and 'دوید' is the final verb 'ran.' The phrase 'بد پیش خسرو' functions as 'toward the presence of Khosrow' — here 'پیش' means 'in the presence of,' and 'بد' is an archaic/poetic form functioning like 'به' ('to/toward').
segments.s688-g113: This clause places the source first (ز بازارگان, 'from the merchants') followed by the past verb بستد (classical past of بستن). In older epic Persian بستن can be used idiomatically for arranging/providing something; here it governs the direct object آن آب گرم ('that warm water'). Word order is source → verb → object, which is a marked but acceptable classical sequence.
segments.s688-g114: بدن ('the body') is the beneficiary/target (object) and تا introduces the purpose clause 'so that'. ندارد is present-tense negative 'does not have/has not', with جهاندار 'the sovereign' as subject placed after the verb — a common classical order where verb precedes subject. شرم is the predicate noun 'shame'.
segments.s688-g115: پس آن مرد بازارگان is a fronted noun phrase 'then that man, the merchant'. پر شتاب is an adjectival/adverbial phrase 'full of haste' (پر = 'full (of)') that modifies the preceding noun phrase; classical Persian often places such modifying phrases after the noun.
segments.s688-g116: می‌آورد is the imperfect/present habitual narrative verb 'he brings/was bringing'. برسان is a verbal complement meaning 'to deliver/bring to' and here functions with می‌آورد to indicate what he brings; روشن گلاب is the direct object 'bright/clear rosewater'. The sequence Verb + verbal complement + object is typical in classical narrative.
segments.s688-g117: دگر باره is an adverbial phrase 'once again/again' (دگر + باره). It is followed immediately by the proper name خراد برزین (name compounded) and then ز راه 'from the road' (i.e. 'coming along the road'); the verbal action is deferred to the next segment, so this fragment sets time, actor and direction.
segments.s688-g118: ازو 'from him' marks source; بستد is the past verb (classical بستن) taking آن جام 'that cup' as its object — in context it means 'took/closed upon' the cup (an idiomatic classical use). The clause continues with و شد نزد شاه where شد (past of شدن) here expresses motion/result 'came/arrived' and نزد شاه 'into the king's presence'. The coordinate conjunction و links the two actions.
segments.s688-g119: This clause uses a somewhat inverted classical order: 'پرستش (worship) پرستنده را (to the worshipper) داشت سود (had benefit)'. را marks the definite object/beneficiary. داشت is past-tense 'had' and سود is the predicate noun 'benefit' — overall: 'worship had benefit for the worshipper.'
segments.s688-g120: بران 'thereby/on that' introduces the result: برتری برتریها is a tautological/genitive-like expression 'superiority of superiorities' (intensifying preeminence). فزود is a classical past 'increased' (3rd pers.), so the whole clause says 'thereby his preeminence increased (above other preeminences)'.
segments.s688-g41: بدو گفت is a classical word order meaning 'to him said' (i.e. 'he said to him'); بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to him/unto him' and گفت is the past-tense reporting verb 'said'. من قیس بن حارثم is a first-person self-identification: من 'I', then the name phrase 'قیس بن حارثم' uses the Arabic patronymic بن (here serving as 'son of') followed by the father's name حارثم. The whole segment functions as direct speech introduced by the reporting clause.
segments.s688-g42: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; آزادگان is the plural 'the free (noble ones)' and عرب specifies they are Arabs ('the free Arabs'). وارثم here behaves as a predicate/appositive meaning 'an heir/descendant' (poetic form, likely corresponding to وارث + -م 'I am' or simply an epithet). Together this gives a short lineage/tribal claim: 'from the free Arabs, (I am) an heir/descendant.'
segments.s688-g43: ز مصر آمدم is a simple past first-person clause: ز 'from' + مصر 'Egypt' + آمدم 'I came'. با یکی کاروان is a prepositional phrase 'with a certain/a certain caravan' (با = with, یکی = a certain/someone, here 'a certain' qualifies کاروان).
segments.s688-g44: برین is the fused form of بر + این (i.e. 'on/over this' or 'of this'); کاروان names the referent 'caravan'. بر منم is literal 'is upon me' (preposition بر + the cliticized predicate منم 'I am'), an idiom meaning 'I have charge of it / it is on me'; ساروان is an appositive title 'caravan‑leader/driver'. The inverted order (object/topic before the predicate) is typical in poetry: 'of this caravan — the caravan lies upon me, (I am) sarvān.'
segments.s688-g45: به آب فراتست is a poetic contraction: به 'by/at' + آب 'water' + فراتست (فرات + است) 'is the Euphrates' — together 'by the water of the Euphrates (is)'. بنگاه من is a possessive phrase 'my station/abode/place' (بنگاه functioning as 'place/establishment' with من 'my'). The copula است is fused into فراتست, a common feature in classical verse.
segments.s688-g46: از انجا = 'from there' (preposition + demonstrative). بدین is the fused form of ب + این (i.e. 'to/into this'); بیشه 'thicket/grove' is the destination/landmark. بد راه من is 'bad road of mine' where بد modifies راه (bad/difficult road) and من is the possessive 'my' placed after the noun in poetic order. The whole clause states that the way from that place to this thicket is bad/difficult for him.
segments.s688-g47: بدو گفت خسروکه = 'to him said Khosrow that...' (بدو گفت = 'he said to him'; خسرو is the speaker's name/title and که introduces the reported question/statement). از خوردنی is a genitive/partitive phrase 'of edible things/food' where خوردنی (adjective 'edible') functions as a noun meaning 'food'.
segments.s688-g48: چه داری is the present interrogative 'what do you have?' (چه = what; داری = you have, present). هم links to the previous item ('and/also'), so هم از چیز گستردنی asks in parallel 'and what (have you) of things to spread/dispense' — از introduces the noun phrase چیز گستردنی, where گستردنی (literally 'spreadable' or 'that can be spread/laid out') stands as a nominalized adjective for goods to be put out/distributed. The clause is a paired question about provisions.
segments.s688-g65: بدان is an adverbial/demonstrative phrase (‘to/unto that’), here introducing the subject phrase. دادگر is a substantive meaning a just one or just ruler; کو is an archaic relative particle meaning ‘who/that’ (common in classical Persian) and links دادگر to the relative clause جهان آفرید (‘created the world’). Word order is noun + relative + verb (world–created), typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s688-g66: توانایی و ناتوان is a coordinated pair (‘the able/able ones and the incapable/powerless ones’). The verb آفرید appears again and governs both coordinated nouns as its objects: He created (آفرید) the able and the unable. The conjunction و simply links the two opposites.
segments.s688-g67: ازان پس is a temporal phrase ‘after that/thereupon’. به یاران is a prepositional phrase ‘to (his) companions’. چنین is an adverb ‘thus’ and گفت is the past-tense verb ‘said’; شاه (king) appears after the verb, reflecting the V–S order frequent in poetry (verb then subject).
segments.s688-g68: This is a reported clause introduced by که (‘that’/‘that whoever…’). هرکس که… is the correlative ‘whoever/anyone who…’; او here is the pronoun ‘he’/‘that one’. بیش دارد گناه literally ‘has more, (he) has sin’ — i.e. ‘whoever has more (of it) has sin/is guilty.’ The verb دارد is present-tense ‘has’ and گناه is the object ‘sin/fault.’
segments.s688-g69: به پیش من is a prepositional expression ‘before me / in my presence / in my sight’ (به + پیش + me). آنکس is the demonstrative noun ‘that person’ as subject. گرامی is an adjective ‘honored/esteemed’ and ترست is a comparative copular form ‘is more’ (a poetic/archaic form placing the copula at the end). Word order places the predicate after the subject, with the comparative copula final.
segments.s688-g70: وزان is a compound connector (و + زان) meaning ‘and from that / and of those’. کهتران here denotes ‘the lesser ones’ (comparative plural); نیز is an adverb ‘also’. نامی is an adjective ‘famous/notable’ and again ترست is the comparative copular ‘is more’—together: ‘and of those lesser ones one is also more famed.’
segments.s688-g71: هرآنکس is ‘whoever/anyone who’. کجا is an archaic/emphatic connective here (literally ‘where’) used in older Persian to introduce the conditional/relative clause; taken together with هرآنکس it means roughly ‘whoever (wherever)’. بیش دارد بدی = ‘has more (of) evil’ — بیش (more) with دارد (has) and بدی (evil/harm) as the thing possessed.
segments.s688-g72: بگشت is a verbal form meaning ‘(he) turns/turns away’ or ‘is turned’ (poetic/past-present usage). The double از … و از … construction repeats the preposition از ‘from’ for emphasis: ‘from me and from the way/path (ره) of wisdom (بخردی).’ بخردی is a genitive/attributive form ‘of wisdom/prudence’, so ره بخردی = ‘the path of wisdom.’
segments.s688-g49: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the content of what they say. ما is the subject 'we'. ماندگانیم is a present-tense nominal predicate formed from the participial noun ماندگان ('the remaining/survivors') plus the 1st‑person plural ending -یم, so literally 'we are (the) survivors'. و هم گرسنه links a second predicate with و ('and') and هم intensifies it as 'also/too'; گرسنه is a predicative adjective ('hungry') with the copula omitted (common in Persian).
segments.s688-g50: This line uses correlative negation: نه ... نه ... ('neither ... nor ...'). توشست here is a contracted/poetic form (توشِ ست) meaning 'there is/to us a tosh (provision)'; the negation نه before it makes 'there is no provision'. ما را uses را after the pronoun in an idiomatic dative/possessive sense ('for us' / 'we have'), and بار و بنه are a pair of nouns meaning 'load / baggage' and 'bundle/pack' respectively — so the clause says that they have neither provisions nor load/baggage.
segments.s688-g51: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (بِ + او contracted), placed before the verb as is common in older/perhaps poetic Persian. گفت is the past tense verb 'said'. تازی is the agent 'the Arab' placed after the verb (verb‑first word order is frequent in narrative). که introduces the reported clause; ایدر ('here') is an adverb of place; بایست (from بایستن) is a modal/auxiliary meaning 'one must / ought to stay' or 'it is necessary to remain' — here it expresses necessity of staying at this place.
segments.s688-g52: مرا is the pronoun with را: in this idiom it functions dative/possessive ('to me' / 'for me'); با تو is the prepositional phrase 'with you' modifying the speaker's attitude. چیز here refers to 'possessions / goods' (used in a general sense), و links the next items, تن 'body' and جان 'life/soul' are listed together as possessions of the self. یکیست is a nominal predicate 'is one' (یکی + است), saying that possessions, body, and life are united/one — i.e. 'for me, with you, my goods, body and life are one.'
segments.s688-g53: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بر here means 'to/toward' (i.e. 'toward the king'); شاه تازی is 'the Arab king' (شاه modifying تازی or vice versa in older word order). بگسترد is the past tense/preterite of گستراندن with the prefix بـ (classical narrative form) meaning 'he spread/extended' — so بگسترد مهر = 'he extended affection/favor'.
segments.s688-g54: بیاورد is the past tense 'he brought' or 'he produced' (bi‑ prefix + آورد). فربه is an adjective 'fat/fattened'. یکی is 'one/a single' and ماده here means 'female' (of an animal). سهر is a noun following ماده; in this line it appears as a descriptive or name; taken together ماده سهر = 'a female of [the kind called] سهر' or 'a vigilant/fretful female' depending on lexical sense. The whole segment says that he brought a fat female (animal).
segments.s688-g55: بکشتند is the past plural 'they killed/slaughtered' (the præfix بـ is classical narrative style). و is 'and'. آتش بر افروختند uses the verb افروختند 'they kindled' with بر indicating 'upon' (they kindled a fire/they set fire alight). Together this gives the sequence of actions: they slaughtered and then kindled the fire.
segments.s688-g56: ترو خشک is a coordinated adjective pair 'green (or moist) and dry' describing the following noun هیزم 'firewood, kindling'. همی‌سوختند is the imperfect/continuous past 'they were burning' (همی‑ prefix marks ongoing action in classical Persian). So the clause says that both green and dry wood was/was being consumed by the fire.

sentences.s688-s65: He commanded that the scribe Ruzbeh write down the name 'Barna' and give it.
sentences.s688-s66: He said to the merchants, 'Farewell; let wisdom be the warp and weft of the heart.'
sentences.s688-s29: They scattered some kebabs on the fire, and the companions hurried to eat.
sentences.s688-s30: They seized and overturned him who was a bad religious scholar; another group hurried to eat.
sentences.s688-s31: They ate plentiful kebab without bread; each chief prepared a place for sleep.
sentences.s688-s32: They slept for a while and then rose; one (of them) composed a new acclamation.
sentences.s688-s13: They rode on until before the River Euphrates; there they saw no trace of vegetation.
sentences.s688-s14: Grown hungry, the men, old and young, saw a grove and running water.
sentences.s688-s15: When Khosrow looked ahead and saw into the grove, he led the army onto that green spot.
sentences.s688-s16: They had become hungry at midday; he fitted (or used) his bow, the game sprang away.
sentences.s688-s5: As soon as he descended, at that very moment a Navand strain came from the skirts of Iran.
sentences.s688-s6: He had a letter from Bahram Chubin; that same letter he kept hidden in his garment.
sentences.s688-s7: He wrote a message to the chief Bahleh: 'If an army should come, do not let them loose.'
sentences.s688-s8: My army is now at the skirts; it will come to your city at any moment.
sentences.s688-s45: Another caravan also appeared on the road, emerging from afar in front of the army.
sentences.s688-s46: A certain wealthy merchant came forward and immediately went up to the king.
sentences.s688-s47: The king said to him, 'Tell me from where you are; where will you go — do you intend to go so swiftly?'
sentences.s688-s48: He said to him, 'From (the place of) Ardashir...' and then: 'I am a certain merchant, a scribe.'
sentences.s688-s1: Khosrow was charging forward into the inner quarters; there was neither water nor any guide.
sentences.s688-s2: He had loosened the reins for that charge and spurred on, but in vain, as far as Bahaleh.
sentences.s688-s3: The city's great ones received him; he was someone of the common people (for ...).
sentences.s688-s4: When Khosrow came near them, they brought down an army upon that city.
sentences.s688-s41: If that is your order, I will bring meat and water on the road; if you do not hurry.
sentences.s688-s42: Khosrow said to him, “There is no counsel except this: that we be with provisions and with a guide.”
sentences.s688-s43: He cast a beast at the Arab onto the road, and thereupon the Arab took the road ahead of the army.
sentences.s688-s44: He kept charging through the desert and mountains, full of hardship and care with that troop.
sentences.s688-s17: They saw something at the spot: there were trees and vegetation and running water.
sentences.s688-s18: At that time a caravan appeared; there were camels, and ahead (or before the inner part) the camel‑drivers.
sentences.s688-s19: When that camel‑driver saw Khosrow's face, he uttered famed praise to him.
sentences.s688-s20: Khosrow said to him, 'What is your name? Where are you going and what do you desire?'
sentences.s688-s49: He asked him, "What name did the one who begot you give you?" He gave this answer: "Mehrān the Praised."
sentences.s688-s50: At that time the king took a provision from him; he said to him, "O renowned chief."

sentences.s688-s52: The king said to him that if a guest is found on the road, the retinue will increase.
sentences.s688-s9: When the elder saw the plan, he at once ran into the presence of Khosrow.
sentences.s688-s10: When Khosrow looked and read the letter, he was left astonished at the affairs of the world.
sentences.s688-s11: They feared that an army would come after him; thereupon the messenger became sorrowful, O king.
sentences.s688-s12: From that city he likewise mounted at that moment; in the midst of the Kayanian host he stopped the charge.
sentences.s688-s61: After that the king said to the merchants, "Which is now the road to the army?"
sentences.s688-s62: Where is your seat in Ardashir's khareh, O hospitable man?
sentences.s688-s63: He said to him, 'O king, with justice and counsel — I am of the merchants and of pure counsel.'
sentences.s688-s64: He told Khosrow each sign in turn; all the secrets were revealed from concealment.
sentences.s688-s37: We ought to have greater hope that you will give him tidings of good throughout.
sentences.s688-s38: The companions raised a cry, 'Bravo! Go on!' and cried, 'O pure‑hearted king, pure in faith.'
sentences.s688-s39: Ask that Arab man which way it is, and how shall I fare with the army?
sentences.s688-s40: He said to him, 'There are over seventy farsangs of desert and mountain before you.'
sentences.s688-s53: The merchants opened the tops of their packs; the moneyed ones came forward from among the dinar‑holders.
sentences.s688-s54: He took the food, sat down on the ground, and kept shouting praise to the king.
sentences.s688-s55: When the food had been eaten, the hospitable man came and seized the cupbearer by the hand.
sentences.s688-s56: When Barzin the Wise saw from afar, he ran from a spot toward Khosrow.
sentences.s688-s57: He obtained warm bathing-water from the merchants so that the sovereign would have no shame.
sentences.s688-s58: Then that merchant, in great haste, brought and delivered bright rosewater.
sentences.s688-s59: Again Kharrad Barzin came along the road, took that cup from him, and went to the king.
sentences.s688-s60: Worship brought benefit to the worshipper; thereby his superiority increased above all other superiorities.
sentences.s688-s21: He said to him, "I am Qays son of Ḥārith, sprung from the free Arabs — an heir/descendant."
sentences.s688-s22: I came from Egypt with a certain caravan; I am the leader of that caravan.
sentences.s688-s23: My station is by the water of the Euphrates; from there to this thicket my road is bad.
sentences.s688-s24: Khosrow said to him, 'What food do you have, and what things to spread?'
sentences.s688-s33: By that Just One who created the world, He created the strong and the weak.
sentences.s688-s34: After that the king said thus to his companions: 'Whoever has more is guilty (has sin).'
sentences.s688-s35: In my sight that person is more honored, and among those lesser ones one is also more famed.
sentences.s688-s36: Whoever has more evil turns away from me and from the path of wisdom.
sentences.s688-s25: That we are the ones left and are also hungry; we have neither provisions nor load or bundle.
sentences.s688-s26: The Arab said to him that one must stay here; 'for me, with you, possessions, body, and life are one.'
sentences.s688-s27: When he showed favor to the Arab king, he brought forth a fat female (animal).
sentences.s688-s28: They slaughtered and kindled a fire; both green and dry firewood burned.
